7.19: “Ho erchomenos” (the coming one) was a recognized messianic title from Ps 118:26 (“blessed is the one who comes in the name of the Lord”), used also at the Triumphal Entry (19:38). 4Q521 (a Qumran fragment sometimes called the Messianic Apocalypse) describes the messianic age bringing healing to the blind, the deaf, and the poor — the precise categories Jesus will enumerate in v.22. John’s question presupposes a specific messianic profile; Jesus’s answer will confirm some elements while reconfiguring others.
7.20: The two disciples as legal witnesses (Deut 19:15: a matter must be established by two or three witnesses) ensures that John’s confirmation of Jesus’s answer carries legal weight: two eyewitnesses can testify to what they heard and saw. The use of the legal-witness structure for a theological inquiry is typical of Luke’s concern for verifiable testimony (asphaleia, certainty, is promised to Theophilus in 1:4). John wants a fact-based confirmation, not a secondhand report.
7.21: “At that very time Jesus cured many” — the response to John’s question is performative and demonstrative rather than propositional: Jesus answers by doing in front of the messengers. The enumeration of healings (diseases, sicknesses, evil spirits, blindness) provides the evidence base for the reply of v.22. This is the only place in the Synoptics where Jesus performs healings specifically for the benefit of witnesses who will report them; the healing becomes testimony.
7.22: The catalogue of messianic signs quotes Isa 35:5-6 (blind see, deaf hear, lame leap, mute sing) and Isa 61:1 (good news for the poor). 4Q521’s list of messianic-age events includes: “he will heal the wounded, give life to the dead, and preach good news to the poor” — a near-exact match to Jesus’s reply. The raising of the dead (not in Isa 35 or 61 but in Isa 26:19) is added; it escalates the list to maximum. John is to understand that the messianic age has arrived even though it does not match every feature of his announced fire-and-judgment vision (3:16-17).
7.23: “Blessed is the one who does not stumble on account of me” (skandalisthē en emoi) — skandalon (stumbling block) was a trap-trigger that springs a snare; to be scandalised by Jesus is to be caught and immobilized by his unexpected form. The beatitude is also a gentle rebuke: John had announced a “coming one” who would baptize with fire and clear the threshing floor (3:16-17); Jesus is baptizing with the Spirit and healing the sick, not judging the wicked. The beatitude asks John to expand his messianic expectations.
7.24: “What did you go out into the wilderness to see?” — the three-question rhetorical structure (reed in the wind, fine clothes, prophet?) invites the crowd to reconsider what they already know about John. The wilderness (erēmos) setting is theologically charged: it is the place of divine testing and preparation (Israel’s 40 years, Elijah’s 40-day journey, Jesus’s 40 days). A reed shaken by the wind (kalamon hypo anemou saleuomenon) is someone who bends to every social pressure — the opposite of John, who stood against Herod and the Pharisees.
7.25: “A man dressed in fine clothes?” — the contrast with John’s camel-hair clothing and leather belt (implied by the Elijah identification, 2 Kgs 1:8) is sharp. “Fine clothes and luxury” (en himatismō endoxō kai tryphe) describe Herod Antipas’s court at Tiberias — John is emphatically not in the royal court but in the royal prison, precisely because he refused to accommodate royal pressure. The question implies: those who bend toward luxury and fine clothes are in palaces, not in prison.
7.26: “More than a prophet” (perissoteron prophētou) — John exceeds the OT prophets not in personal holiness but in salvation-historical position: the prophets predicted a future they did not see; John stands at the threshold, personally introducing the one they predicted. The same logic appears in Hebrews 11:39-40 (the OT saints did not receive what was promised; they were waiting for “us”). John’s unique position (last of the old order, herald of the new) makes him greater in prophetic role while lesser in covenant position than the least disciple who stands fully in the new age.
7.27: The Malachi 3:1 citation (“I will send my messenger ahead of you, who will prepare your way before you”) is quoted in modified form: the original has God speaking in first person about his own coming; Luke’s version reattributes it as God speaking to Jesus (“before you”). This subtle shift is a high Christological claim embedded in a citation: what was said about God’s forerunner is now applied to Jesus’s forerunner. Malachi 4:5 identified this forerunner as Elijah — hence the later tradition identifying John as Elijah-redivivus (Matt 11:14; 17:12).
7.28: “Among those born of women there is no one greater than John” — the Semitic idiom “born of women” (gennētos gynaikōn) means a mortal human being (cf. Job 14:1: “Mortals, born of woman, live but a few days”); the phrase places John at the pinnacle of human history as God’s last and greatest herald. “Yet the least in the kingdom is greater than he” — the kingdom introduces a new order of privilege: even the newest member of the kingdom stands on the far side of the watershed event John could only announce, thereby having received more than John possessed.
7.29: “All the people, even the tax collectors, when they heard Jesus’s words, acknowledged that God’s way was right” (edikaiosān ton theon, they justified/declared-righteous God) — by receiving John’s baptism, the people publicly acknowledged the validity of his prophetic call and thus vindicated God’s sending of John. The pairing of “all the people” and “even the tax collectors” creates the broad populist response to set against the elite rejection: tax collectors (telōnai) were generally despised as Roman collaborators and social outcasts, yet they responded where the religious leaders did not.
7.30: “The Pharisees and the experts in the law rejected God’s purpose for themselves” (tēn boulēn tou theou ēthetēsan) — boulē (plan, counsel, purpose) is a major Lukan concept for the divine plan that encompasses the entire salvation history: Acts 2:23 (Jesus delivered up “by God’s deliberate plan and foreknowledge”); Acts 4:28 (the passion fulfills what God’s hand and plan had decided). Rejecting John’s baptism was not merely a personal religious choice but a refusal of participation in the divine salvation-plan — with consequences that unfold throughout Luke-Acts.
7.31: “To what shall I compare the people of this generation?” — the parable of the children in the market-place (agora) addresses the inconsistency of the religious leaders who rejected both John and Jesus on contradictory grounds. The “generation” (genea) is Luke’s consistent term for the resistant religious leadership (cf. 9:41; 11:29-32,50-51; 17:25; 21:32); it does not mean everyone alive at the time but the contemporaneous leadership who reject the prophets.
7.32: “We played the pipe for you and you did not dance / we sang a dirge and you did not weep” — children’s playing of wedding and funeral games was a common sight in the agora (marketplace, the social center of town life). The parable describes the perverse inconsistency of the generation: it refused to celebrate when celebration was called for (John’s fast = mourning-game refused) and refused to mourn when mourning was called for (Jesus’s feast = wedding-game refused). No response to God’s messengers is deemed appropriate; the problem is not the form of the message but the heart of the hearers.
7.33: “John the Baptist came neither eating bread nor drinking wine” — John’s abstinence from bread and wine may reflect a Nazirite vow (1:15: no wine or strong drink; Num 6:3-4 prohibits the Nazirite from wine and all grape products). The accusation of demon-possession (daimonion echei) was the default charge against those whose behavior was inexplicable by normal social categories; the Beelzebul controversy (11:14-22) will repeat the pattern with Jesus himself. Abnormal behavior = supernatural causation; the question is which supernatural agent.
7.34: “The Son of Man came eating and drinking” — the contrast between John’s asceticism and Jesus’s table-fellowship is historical and theological: Jesus ate with sinners, tax collectors, Pharisees, and crowds, making table-fellowship a primary medium of the kingdom. The epithet thrown at him (“glutton and drunkard” = phagos kai oinopotēs) echoes Deut 21:20’s description of the rebellious son: “This son of ours is stubborn and rebellious; he is a glutton and a drunkard.” The Deuteronomic rebellious son was stoned; Jesus is accused of the same pattern.
7.35: “Wisdom is proved right by all her children” — the personification of divine Wisdom (Sophia) as a figure who sends her agents (Prov 9:3; Sir 24; Wis 7:24-27) is the implicit framework: John and Jesus are Wisdom’s messengers or children, and their vindication comes not from the generation’s acceptance but from the fruit of their lives. Q 7:35 has “her children” where Matthew 11:19 has “her deeds”; Luke’s version identifies John and Jesus as Wisdom’s children — they prove Wisdom right by their ministries.
7.36: “One of the Pharisees invited Jesus to have dinner with him” (katelithe) — reclining at dinner (kataklinō) was the posture of the Greco-Roman formal meal: diners reclined on low couches arranged around a central table. Feet extended away from the table, which is why the woman stands behind Jesus at his feet (v.38). Pharisee dinner invitations to Jesus appear three times in Luke (7:36; 11:37; 14:1) — the relationship between Jesus and the Pharisees is not simple mutual hostility but a complex mixture of interest, critique, and occasional fellowship.
7.37: “A woman in that town who had lived a sinful life” (hamartōlos) — in the context of a formal dinner, the identification as a known sinner (likely a prostitute, the most common reference for women publicly identified as sinners in this period) makes her presence at the dinner a severe social transgression. Formal dinner parties in antiquity were gender-segregated for respectable women; a prostitute’s appearance would have been scandalous to the assembled guests. Her bringing an alabaster jar of myron (expensive perfume/ointment) suggests some degree of financial means.
7.38: “Began to wet his feet with her tears, and wiped them with her hair” — the sequence of actions (tears, hair-wiping, kissing feet, perfume) escalates from spontaneous grief to deliberate honor. Hair-letting in public was generally associated with mourning or disreputable women; anointing feet rather than the head was a reversal of normal honor (hosts anointed guests’ heads, not feet). The woman’s acts are excessive, counter-cultural, and deeply personal; they constitute a sustained act of devotion that social convention makes scandalous.
7.39: “If this man were a prophet, he would know who is touching him” — Simon’s internal reasoning assumes that prophetic knowledge includes discernment of ritual status. The prophet’s ability to perceive hidden realities (cf. 2 Kgs 5:25-27, Elisha knowing Gehazi’s secret) would, in Simon’s framework, include knowing the woman’s sinful status and therefore withdrawing from her touch. Jesus’s apparent unawareness seems to Simon like prophetic failure — evidence that he is not the prophet he is claimed to be.
7.40: Jesus’s address to Simon (“I have something to tell you”) takes the form of the parable-introduction: the teacher invites the student into a thought experiment rather than issuing a direct rebuke. Simon’s response (Didaskale, Teacher) is respectful; the social dynamic is host-honored-guest, not hostile opponent. Jesus’s parabolic method here — as throughout Luke — is to lead the hearer into self-conviction through a story they can evaluate neutrally before its application lands on them.
7.41: “A certain moneylender” (danistēs) — the lending industry occupied a complex legal space in Jewish society. Torah prohibited interest between Jews (Exod 22:25; Lev 25:36-37; Deut 23:19-20) but permitted it with Gentiles. The Hillel-era prosbul was a legal instrument allowing loans near the Sabbatical year without the automatic debt-release; it effectively enabled a lending economy. Five hundred denarii (approximately two years’ wages for a day laborer) vs. fifty denarii (approximately two months’ wages) creates a 10:1 ratio for the debt comparison.
7.42: “He forgave the debts of both” (echarisato amphoterois) — the verb charizomai (to graciously give, to forgive) is used for both commercial debt-cancellation and divine forgiveness of sin; the word play drives the parable’s logic. The Jubilee principle of Deut 15:1-11 (release of debts every seventh year) and Lev 25 (the full Jubilee every 49/50 years) is the biblical background for understanding debt-forgiveness as a theological concept: God’s forgiveness is the ultimate Jubilee cancellation of debt.
7.43: Simon’s “I suppose” (hypolambanō) indicates reluctant assent; the logic is inescapable but the conclusion will be uncomfortable. Jesus confirms the judgment (“you have judged correctly”) — using the very act of judging correctly about the parable to position Simon as the judge of his own behavior toward the woman. The parable-as-trap structure is typical of Nathan’s parable to David (2 Sam 12:1-7): the story draws a judgment from the listener that then applies to themselves.
7.44: “Do you see this woman?” (blepeis tautēn tēn gynaika) — the three contrast-pairs (water for feet / tears; greeting kiss / continuous foot-kissing; oil for head / perfume for feet) enumerate the standard hospitality courtesies of the period. Providing water for a guest’s feet-washing was the minimum hospitality (Gen 18:4; 1 Sam 25:41); a kiss of greeting (philēma) was the standard welcome; anointing the head with oil (Ps 23:5) was a gesture of honor. Simon provided none of these; the woman provided more than all of them.
7.45: “You did not give me a kiss” — the hospitality kiss (philēma) was the standard greeting between men of equal or near-equal social status in the Mediterranean world. Simon’s failure to greet Jesus with a kiss may indicate that he regarded Jesus as socially inferior (a Galilean craftsman/teacher without official credentials) despite the dinner invitation. Or it may be simple negligence. Either way, his omission is contrasted with the woman’s ou dieleipen kataphilousa (she has not stopped kissing — continuous, intense kissing of the feet).
7.46: “You did not put oil on my head, but she poured perfume on my feet” — the inversion of expected locations (head vs. feet) underscores the woman’s excessive reversal of honor-protocol: she honors the lowest part with the most expensive substance. Elaion (olive oil, used for normal anointing) vs. myron (costly perfume/ointment imported from the East) — the woman’s choice of myron and its application to the feet is a conspicuously inverted act of extravagant devotion, comparable to Mary of Bethany’s anointing (John 12:3).
7.47: “Her many sins have been forgiven — as her great love has shown” — the cause-effect direction is clarified by the parable: forgiveness precedes and produces love; love is the evidence of forgiveness already given, not the merit that earns it. The Greek construction (hoti ēgapēsen poly = because she loved much) can mean either “she is forgiven because she loved much” (love = basis) or “how much she loved is the proof that she has been forgiven much” (love = evidence). The parable of vv.41-42 settles the interpretation: the greater the forgiven debt, the greater the resulting love.
7.48: “Your sins are forgiven” (aphiontai sou hai hamartiai) — the present passive indicative: they stand forgiven, the forgiveness is in effect. The public pronouncement in the Pharisee’s house parallels the private pronouncement to the paralytic (5:20: aphiontai soi hai hamartiai sou). In both cases the announcement is public and the controversy focuses on who has the authority to pronounce it. The synagogue/dining room is the social venue of maximum impact: the forgiveness is declared where shame was publicly attached.
7.49: “Who is this who even forgives sins?” (tis houtos estin hos kai hamartias aphiēsin) — the guests’ question echoes the scribes’ internal questioning at 5:21 (“Who is this who speaks blasphemies? Who can forgive sins but God alone?”). The Christological question is raised explicitly but left unanswered — Luke’s narrative technique of embedding the right question for the reader to supply. Only God forgives sins; Jesus forgives sins; therefore — the reader must draw the conclusion the text withholds.
7.50: “Your faith has saved you; go in peace” (hē pistis sou sesōken se) — the formula of dismissal (poreuou eis eirēnēn) echoes Eli’s dismissal of Hannah (1 Sam 1:17: “Go in peace”) and the standard Hebrew blessing. Sesōken (has saved you, perfect tense: the salvation stands complete) encompasses forgiveness, restoration, and social rehabilitation — the comprehensive shalom of the Jubilee year. The woman departs as a complete person, not merely as a forgiven sinner: she has received the full liberation that Jesus announced in Isa 61:1 (4:18).

10.25: The parable of the Good Samaritan (10:25-37) is Luke's unique contribution to the NT. The lawyer's question (who is my neighbor?) is a genuine Second Temple debate: some interpretations limited neighbor to fellow Jews or even only to Pharisees; others extended it to proselytes. The Samaritan as hero is the most provocative element: Samaritans and Jews had long-standing mutual hostility (John 4:9, Josephus Ant. 18.30 describes violent incidents). The parable does not define who counts as a neighbor (the expected answer) but asks which of the three acted as a neighbor — shifting the question from classification (who is in my neighbor-category?) to disposition (how do I neighbor?). The priest and Levite's failure is probably a purity concern: contact with a potentially dead body would render them unclean for temple service.
10.1: The mission of the seventy-two (or seventy, depending on manuscript tradition -- P75, B, D read seventy; majority text reads seventy-two) deliberately invokes two OT precedents. Seventy is the number of elders Moses appointed (Num 11:16-17, 24-25) and the number of nations in the Table of Nations (Gen 10 MT = 70; LXX = 72 nations). Seventy-two aligns with the LXX count. Luke's choice positions the expanded mission as both a new-Moses leadership structure and an anticipation of the universal Gentile mission that Acts will narrate. Sending in pairs (ana duo) follows the Jewish legal principle that testimony requires two witnesses (Deut 17:6; 19:15) and was the practice of later rabbinical emissaries (m. Sanhedrin 1:1-3).
10.2: "The harvest is plentiful, but the workers are few" -- the harvest metaphor for the gathering of souls appears in Jewish eschatology (Isa 27:12: "the Lord will thresh... one by one"; Joel 3:13: "swing the sickle, for the harvest is ripe"; 4 Ezra 4:28-32). In the OT, the harvest of nations was often an image of divine judgment; Jesus reappropriates it as the gathering of the saved. The prayer to the Lord of the harvest (the Lord as owner of the crop, who dispatches workers) frames mission as response to divine initiative: God is already working; the disciples are sent to participate, not to originate.
10.3: "Go! I am sending you out like lambs among wolves" -- the vulnerability contrast (lamb/wolf) was proverbial in antiquity. In the OT, the sheep among wolves image described Israel among hostile nations (Ezek 22:27; Jer 50:17; Zeph 3:3). Here it is the disciples among hostile audiences. The shaliach principle (m. Berakhot 5:5: "a person's agent is as the person himself") governs the mission: to receive the disciples is to receive Jesus; to reject them is to reject him (v.16). The lambs being sent implies the shepherd's awareness and responsibility for the lambs' vulnerability.
10.4: "Do not take a purse or bag or sandals" -- the travel instructions mirror the Passover night (Exod 12:11: "with your cloak tucked in, sandals on your feet, staff in hand"), conveying urgency. "Do not greet anyone on the road" echoes Elisha's instruction to Gehazi (2 Kgs 4:29: "if you meet anyone, do not greet him; if anyone greets you, do not answer"), signaling single-minded focus on the urgent mission. In Oriental culture, greetings could involve lengthy exchange of formal courtesies; bypassing them signals the urgency of the kingdom's arrival.
10.5: "When you enter a house, first say, 'Peace to this house'" -- Shalom (Peace) was the standard Jewish greeting (1 Sam 25:6; Dan 10:19). In the mission context, however, the greeting is not merely social formula but a prophetic word that carries its own power: if a "son of peace" (ben shalom in Aramaic = someone characterized by peace, receptive to the gospel) is there, the peace will rest on the household. The "son of peace" idiom is a Semitic idiom for a person whose essential character is described by the noun: "son of wisdom," "son of perdition," "son of the resurrection" (20:36).
10.6: "A son of peace" (huios eirēnēs) is a Lukan Semitism: huios + genitive noun denotes a person characterized by the noun. The dynamic quality of the peace-greeting -- it "rests on" the receptive person but "returns to you" if rejected -- reflects the understanding of the prophetic word as active (cf. Isa 55:11: "my word... will not return to me empty"). The mission is not a conditional offer but an active force: peace is released by the greeting and either received (and stays) or rejected (and returns to the sender).
10.7: "Stay in that house, eating and drinking whatever they give you, for the worker deserves his wages" -- the prohibition on moving from house to house prevented the appearance of shopping for better accommodations (an honor issue in Mediterranean culture) and prevented the disciples from becoming a burden that exhausted hospitality. "The worker deserves his wages" appears also in 1 Tim 5:18 and 1 Cor 9:14, suggesting it was a recognized principle in early Christianity. The Didache 11-12 later grapples with the problem of traveling prophets who abuse hospitality -- the Lukan instruction anticipates this concern.
10.8: "Eat what is set before you" -- this instruction has been interpreted as a practical suspension of Jewish food laws when staying in Gentile homes, anticipating the Acts 10-11 vision (Peter's sheet vision). In Jewish law, eating with Gentiles risked consuming forbidden foods; the instruction allows the mission to proceed without imposing Jewish dietary observance on hosts. Alternatively, it may simply mean not being fastidious within Jewish contexts. Either way, it subordinates ritual concerns to mission priority, a pattern Paul later develops (1 Cor 10:27: "eat whatever is set before you").
10.9: "Heal the sick who are there and tell them, 'The kingdom of God has come near to you'" -- the pairing of healing and proclamation defines the mission: the deeds confirm the words, and the words interpret the deeds. "The kingdom of God has come near" (ēggiken eph' hymas = has come upon you) is the closest Luke comes to the Markan "the kingdom of God is at hand." The healings are not merely humanitarian acts but signs of the kingdom's presence (cf. 11:20: "if I drive out demons by the finger of God, the kingdom of God has come upon you").
10.10: When a town does not receive the disciples, the prophetic-sign of wiping off the dust (v.11) is performed publicly in the town streets. This is not mere personal rejection but communal rejection, carrying communal consequences. The town-square witness maximizes the public character of the act: the whole community can see the sign and understand the declaration that the kingdom has been offered and refused.
10.11: "Even the dust of your town we wipe from our feet as a warning to you" -- Jews returning from Gentile lands wiped the dust from their feet to avoid bringing Gentile impurity into the Holy Land (m. Oholot 2:3 and later sources). The gesture here reverses the direction: the disciples shake off the dust of the rejecting town, treating it as profane territory. "Yet be sure of this: the kingdom of God has come near" -- the warning is that the kingdom's nearness makes the rejection more serious, not less.
10.12: "It will be more bearable for Sodom on that day than for that town" -- Sodom was the paradigm of divine judgment on the most wicked city (Gen 19; Isa 1:9-10; Ezek 16:46-50). Sodom's notorious wickedness (Gen 18:20: "the outcry against Sodom and Gomorrah is so great") makes its sentence a measuring standard. Jesus' claim that Galilean towns that reject the kingdom will face harsher judgment than Sodom is shocking: the privilege of having heard the gospel makes rejection more culpable, not less.
10.13: Chorazin and Bethsaida were Galilean towns near Capernaum. Chorazin (modern Khirbet Kerazeh, 3 km north of Capernaum) was excavated to reveal a fourth-century CE synagogue, probably built on an earlier site. Bethsaida (modern et-Tell near the Jordan's entry into the lake) was the hometown of Philip, Andrew, and Peter (John 1:44; 12:21). Both towns apparently witnessed significant miracles not recorded in the Gospels. The comparison cities -- Tyre and Sidon -- were Phoenician port cities condemned repeatedly in OT prophecy (Isa 23; Ezek 26-28; Joel 3:4-8; Amos 1:9-10) as paradigms of pride and wealth.
10.14: Tyre and Sidon represent the epitome of Gentile paganism -- proud commercial cities that forgot God (Ezek 28:2: "Because you think you are wise, as wise as a god"). The OT prophets devoted extended oracles to their judgment. That these pagan cities would have repented in sackcloth and ashes (the garments of mourning and penitence: Est 4:1; Dan 9:3; Jonah 3:6) if they had seen Chorazin's miracles underscores both the adequacy of the revelation given to the Galilean towns and their culpable failure to respond to it.
10.15: Capernaum's special condemnation -- "will you be lifted up to heaven? No, you will go down to Hades" -- echoes the oracle against the king of Babylon in Isa 14:13-15 ("you said in your heart, 'I will ascend to heaven... I will raise my throne above the stars of God'... But you will be brought down to the realm of the dead"). Capernaum was the base of Jesus' Galilean ministry and the site of his greatest miracles; its judgment is correspondingly severe. The Hades reference (Hadēs = the realm of the dead) reflects the OT Sheol tradition, contrasted with heaven as the place of divine favor.
10.16: "Whoever listens to you listens to me; whoever rejects you rejects me; but whoever rejects me rejects him who sent me" -- the shaliach (agent) principle in its fullest form. In Jewish law, the principle kishăluḥo kōmotō ("as his sender, so the one sent") meant the agent's actions had full legal force as the principal's own actions (m. Berakhot 5:5; b. Kiddushin 41b-43a). Jesus applies this to the disciples: reception/rejection of them is reception/rejection of him, which is reception/rejection of the Father. The chain of divine sending (Father -- Son -- disciples) makes the mission cosmic in significance.
10.17: "The seventy-two returned with joy and said: 'Lord, even the demons submit to us in your name!'" -- the disciples' report focuses on the unexpected: not healing or welcome but authority over demons. The authority over demons was given in 9:1 and 10:17-19; the specific mention of Jesus' name reflects the Second Temple Jewish practice of invoking the name of divine figures in exorcism (Acts 19:13: "In the name of Jesus whom Paul preaches"). The "name" (onoma) represents the authority, character, and power of the person; to act in Jesus' name is to act as his authorized agent.
10.18: "I saw Satan fall like lightning from heaven" -- Luke's unique logion. The background is complex: Isa 14:12 LXX ("how you have fallen from heaven, O morning star, son of the dawn") was applied to a primordial fall of Satan/Lucifer in Jewish and later Christian interpretation (1 Enoch 86-88 describes angels falling as stars; Rev 12:9 narrates Satan's expulsion from heaven). Jesus' vision -- whether of a primordial event or a present cosmic consequence of the disciples' mission -- frames the exorcisms as battles in a cosmic war. The disciples' victories on earth have heavenly significance: each exorcism is a blow to the enemy's domain.
10.19: "I have given you authority to trample on snakes and scorpions and to overcome all the power of the enemy" -- snakes and scorpions as symbols of demonic danger appear in Ps 91:13 ("you will tread on the lion and the cobra; you will trample the great lion and the serpent") and Deut 8:15 (the wilderness "with its venomous snakes and scorpions"). The authority given is not just over physical dangers but over the full power of the enemy -- the spiritual forces behind them. Acts 28:3-6 (Paul bitten by a viper but unharmed) and Acts 16:16-18 (exorcism of the fortune-telling spirit) show this authority in practice.
10.20: "Rejoice that your names are written in heaven" -- the heavenly book of life appears in Exod 32:32-33 (Moses asks God not to blot him from the book), Dan 12:1 ("everyone whose name is found written in the book"), Ps 69:28 ("erase them from the book of life"), and Rev 3:5; 20:12. The Lukan reassurance shifts the disciples' joy from their performance (exorcisms) to their standing (enrollment in God's register). The victory over demons is real but contingent; the permanent enrollment in the heavenly record is the firmer ground of joy.
10.21: The prayer of jubilation -- "I praise you, Father, Lord of heaven and earth, because you have hidden these things from the wise and learned, and revealed them to little children" -- echoes Isa 29:14 ("the wisdom of the wise will perish, the intelligence of the intelligent will vanish") and Sir 51:23-27. "Little children" (nēpiois = infants, those without wisdom or status) as the recipients of revelation contrasts with the sophos kai synetos (wise and understanding = the scribal elite). The Qumran community similarly restricted the mysteries to the elect (1QS 11:3-8; 1QH 12:27-28). Luke's "at that time Jesus, full of joy through the Holy Spirit" (agralliasāto in the Holy Spirit) underscores the Trinitarian character of the moment.
10.22: "All things have been committed to me by my Father" -- the most Johannine-sounding logion in the Synoptics (cf. John 3:35; 13:3; 17:2). Matthew 11:27 has the same saying. It is sometimes called the "Johannine thunderbolt" for its resemblance to the Fourth Gospel's high Christology. The claim to exclusive mutual knowledge ("no one knows who the Son is except the Father, and no one knows who the Father is except the Son") establishes a relationship of unique intimacy that grounds the mission's authority: what the disciples receive from Jesus is not one teacher's insight but the self-revelation of the one who uniquely knows the Father.
10.23: The private beatitude to disciples (vv.23-24, parallel in Matt 13:16-17) is addressed specifically to "his disciples" (tous mathētas kat' idian = to the disciples privately). The blessedness is not for performance but for historical location: to be living in the generation of fulfillment, to see what prophets and kings longed to see (Dan 2:28: "there is a God in heaven who reveals mysteries, and he has shown King Nebuchadnezzar what will happen in days to come"). The prophets who predicted the Servant's suffering and glory wanted to understand what they predicted (1 Pet 1:10-12).
10.24: "I tell you that many prophets and kings wanted to see what you see but did not see it, and to hear what you hear but did not hear it" -- the prophets who foresaw the messianic age (Isa 61; Dan 9; Mic 5:2) and the kings of the Davidic line who hoped for the ultimate Davidic heir (Ps 2; 72; 110) were anticipating what the disciples now witness directly. The privilege is not of the disciples' deserving but of their moment: they live in the fullness of time (Gal 4:4: "when the set time had fully come"). This verse is the hermeneutical key for Luke's use of the OT: the whole prophetic tradition was anticipating this.
10.26: The lawyer's question "What is written in the Law? How do you read it?" shifts the burden back to the questioner. In rabbinic discussion, citing Scripture (ma katuv = what is written) followed by interpretation (kiv'an de katib = how do you read it?) was the standard pedagogical method. Jesus does not simply answer the question but draws out the lawyer's own knowledge, making him the source of the correct answer. This is consistent with the Socratic method of Jesus' teaching (also Luke 10:36: "Which of these three do you think was a neighbor?").
10.27: The lawyer combines Deut 6:5 (love God with all heart/soul/strength -- the Shema) and Lev 19:18 (love your neighbor as yourself). In Matthew 22:34-40 and Mark 12:28-34, it is Jesus who combines these two commandments; in Luke 10:27, the lawyer makes the combination himself -- Luke's version emphasizes the lawyer's own correct understanding, which Jesus affirms. The Shema (Deut 6:4-9) was recited twice daily in Jewish prayer; Lev 19:18 was recognized by Rabbi Akiva (b. Sifra 89b) as "the great principle of the Torah." The combination thus represents the highest insight of Jewish ethical reflection.
10.28: "You have answered correctly. Do this and you will live" -- Jesus' affirmation of the lawyer's answer echoes Lev 18:5 ("keep my decrees and laws, for the person who obeys them will live by them") and Ezek 20:11 ("I gave them my decrees and made known to them my laws, by which the person who obeys them will live"). The "do this" is the operative clause: the lawyer knows the commandment but does not do it -- hence the follow-up question "who is my neighbor?" which is an attempt at self-justification (v.29) rather than genuine inquiry.
10.29: "And who is my neighbor?" -- the lawyer's self-justifying question (thelōn dikaioōsai heauton = wishing to justify himself) reveals that the real question is not theoretical but practical: he is looking for the minimum definition of "neighbor" that allows him to declare his obligation fulfilled. The Second Temple debate on who counts as a "neighbor" was real: some interpretations limited the neighbor-obligation to fellow Jews, or even to members of one's own Pharisaic group (haverim = associates who observe tithing purity laws). The parable Jesus tells does not answer the definitional question but reframes it entirely (v.36).
10.30: The Jerusalem-to-Jericho road was a real, well-known 27 km descent of approximately 900 meters through the Judean wilderness. It was notorious for banditry: Josephus mentions it (War 4.474); Jerome called it the "Bloody Way" or "Ascent of Adummim" (Adummim = blood-red, from the red rock color -- some identify this with the brigand-haunted pass). The victim "fell among robbers" (peripesen lēstais) -- the same verb as "fell" in Ezekiel's allegory of Jerusalem (Ezek 16:15; lēstēs = bandit/robber, the same word used of Barabbas and the crucified alongside Jesus in 23:18-19, 39). The attack was total: stripped, beaten, left half-dead (hēmithane = half-dead, barely alive).
10.31: A priest happened to be going down the road. The priest's failure to help has been most plausibly explained by the concern for ritual impurity: contact with a corpse (or a potentially dead person) would render a priest unclean for seven days (Lev 21:1-4; Num 19:11-13). During the period of uncleanness, he could not perform temple service. Some interpreters have argued that the priest was traveling away from Jerusalem (going down to Jericho) and therefore not at risk of immediately serving; others note that a priest could own property in Jericho and needed to maintain ritual purity for future service. Either way, the risk-calculation of purity-maintenance over compassion is the narrative's indictment.
10.32: A Levite came to the same place and passed by on the other side. Levites served in the temple in subordinate roles to priests (musicians, doorkeepers, assistants) and were also subject to purity regulations, though less strictly than priests (Lev 21:1-4 applies specifically to Aaronide priests; Levites had fewer restrictions). The Levite's passing mirrors the priest's: he "saw him" (idōn) and then passed by on the other side (antipēlthen). The deliberate action (passing on the other side rather than simply continuing) implies active avoidance rather than mere inattention.
10.33: A Samaritan traveler came upon the man. The audience would have expected the third figure in the priest-Levite-third sequence to be an Israelite layman. Instead, Jesus introduces a Samaritan -- the most despised group from a Jewish perspective. Samaritans were descended from the mixed population settled in the Northern Kingdom after the Assyrian deportation (2 Kgs 17:24-41) and had developed a distinct religious identity (the Pentateuch only, worship on Mt. Gerizim, rejection of the Jerusalem temple). Jewish-Samaritan tensions were intense (John 4:9: "Jews do not associate with Samaritans"; Josephus Ant. 18.29-30 describes violent incidents on the Galilee-Jerusalem pilgrimage route). The Samaritan hero makes the parable maximally provocative.
10.34: The Samaritan's care is described in extended detail: he "took pity on him" (espágchnisśthē = was moved in his bowels/compassion), went to him, bandaged his wounds, poured oil and wine (oil as soothing agent, wine as antiseptic -- basic ancient wound care), put him on his own donkey, brought him to an inn, and took care of him. Each action represents an escalation of personal involvement and cost. The inn (pandocheion = an establishment that received everyone) was the common lodging facility on major roads; the term pandochos (host of all) reflects the universal hospitality that the inn offers -- in ironic contrast to the priest and Levite who offered none.
10.35: The next day, the Samaritan gave the innkeeper two denarii (approximately two days' wages for a laborer) and promised to reimburse any additional expenses on his return. The financial commitment is open-ended: "whatever you spend in addition, I will repay you when I return." This is not charity with a fixed limit but unlimited obligation assumed by the Samaritan on behalf of a stranger from an ethnic group hostile to his own. The return journey implies the Samaritan was a regular traveler on this route -- a merchant or tradesman who would pass through again.
10.36: "Which of these three do you think was a neighbor to the man who fell into the hands of robbers?" -- the question inverts the lawyer's original question. The lawyer asked: "Who counts as my neighbor?" (seeking to define and limit his obligation). Jesus asks: "Which one acted as a neighbor?" (shifting from definition to disposition). The answer cannot be the "Samaritan" (an answer the lawyer could not bring himself to say; he says "the one who had mercy on him"). The redefinition: a neighbor is not a category of person to whom I owe obligations but a quality of relationship I establish by my action. Neighborliness is not determined by who the other person is but by how I choose to act.
10.37: "Go and do likewise" (poreuou kai sy poiei homōios) -- the imperative that ends the parable is both personal (sy = you) and present-continuous (poiei = keep doing). The parable is not an inspiring story about Samaritan goodness but a direct challenge to the lawyer's existing practice. The neighbor he failed to be is the neighbor he is now called to become -- not toward some abstract other person but in the next moment of encountering a person in need. The "likewise" (homōios) echoes the word used in the lawyer's own summary of the second commandment (homōios in v.28 is implicit; the pattern is the Samaritan's pattern).
10.38: "A village" without a name in Luke (John 11:1 identifies it as Bethany, 3 km east of Jerusalem on the Jericho road). The identification of Bethany as Mary and Martha's home is supported by John 11-12, where the same sisters appear with their brother Lazarus. The episode is unique to Luke, serving as the pairing narrative for the Good Samaritan (action) and the Mary/Martha encounter (contemplation). The two women represent different but complementary responses to Jesus: Martha's diakonia (service/hospitality) and Mary's listening at his feet (the learner's posture).
10.39: Mary "sat at the Lord's feet listening to what he said" -- sitting at a teacher's feet was the posture of the disciple (Acts 22:3: Paul "educated at the feet of Gamaliel"). Women normally did not function as disciples in Jewish educational settings; the Mishnah records debates about teaching Torah to women (m. Sotah 3:4: "Let the words of the Torah be burned rather than handed over to women" -- the minority opinion of Rabbi Eliezer, opposed by others). Jesus' acceptance of Mary's discipleship role (and his defense of it in v.42) is a counter-cultural act of inclusion.
10.40: Martha was "distracted by all the preparations" (perispaō = pulled around, scattered) -- the Greek suggests her attention was being pulled in multiple directions by the work of hosting. Her appeal to Jesus to tell Mary to help reflects both the social obligation (the host's honor depended on adequate hospitality) and the expectation that Mary should be performing her domestic duty. "Don't you care?" (ou melei soi) is a direct address that implies Jesus is at fault for the situation by allowing Mary to sit rather than help. This is the only time in the Gospels that someone rebukes Jesus for what he allows.
10.41: "Martha, Martha" -- the repeated name of address appears at moments of tender urgency in the NT: "Simon, Simon" (22:31), "Saul, Saul" (Acts 9:4). The doubling in Jewish address can indicate deep emotion, urgency, or affection. "You are worried and upset about many things" -- the critique of Martha is not that hospitality is wrong but that anxiety (merimnās = worry/anxiety) and being upset (thorybazē = troubled/disturbed) about the many preparations reveals a misplaced priority. The worry itself -- not the service -- is the problem. The contrast with Mary is not service vs. leisure but anxious distraction vs. focused attention.
10.42: "But few things are needed -- or indeed only one" -- the textual tradition varies: some MSS read "one thing is needed"; others read "few things are needed, or one" (a conflation). The point is the contrast between the "many things" of Martha's preparation and the "one thing" or "few things" that actually matter. "Mary has chosen what is better" (tēn agathēn merida = the good portion) and "it will not be taken away from her" (using the same root as the estate-division imagery). The "good portion" echoes Ps 16:5-6 (God as portion), the Levitical tradition (God as the Levites' portion, Num 18:20), and the wisdom tradition where the sage's portion is wisdom itself (Wis 8:7). The contemplative life is not superior to the active but is the necessary ground of it.

15.1: The three parables of Luke 15 (lost sheep, lost coin, lost son) are unique to Luke and form a unified trilogy on the divine initiative in seeking and finding the lost. The social context of v.1-2 is critical: the Pharisees and scribes murmur because Jesus receives (accepts, welcomes, eats with) sinners. Table fellowship was a community boundary marker in first-century Judaism: with whom you ate defined who you included. The parable trilogy is Jesus's theological defense of his boundary-transgressing practice: God himself seeks the lost (lost sheep, lost coin) and runs to embrace the returning prodigal — therefore the community formed around this God should reflect the same excess of seeking, welcoming love.
15.2: “This man welcomes sinners and eats with them” (prosdechetai hamartōlous kai synesthiei autois) — the Pharisees’ complaint is not merely moral but social and theological: eating with someone was a declaration of community inclusion. In Second Temple Judaism, table fellowship was a community boundary marker; sharing a meal communicated shared purity status and social solidarity. By eating with “sinners” (those who did not observe Pharisaic halakhah), Jesus is including them in his fellowship without requiring the repentance and restoration process the Pharisees considered necessary.
15.3: “He told them this parable” — the parable trilogy of ch. 15 is Jesus’s direct theological response to the Pharisees’ complaint of v.2. The three parables function together as a threefold argument for the divine initiative in seeking and the appropriateness of celebrating the found. The progression (100 sheep, 10 coins, two sons) moves from the smallest to the most complex loss, and from the impersonal (sheep) to the personal (son).
15.4: The shepherd leaving the ninety-nine to search for the one lost sheep — the shepherd-flock imagery for God’s relationship to Israel pervades the OT: Ps 23, Ezek 34 (the extended critique of Israel’s bad shepherds and God’s promise to be the good shepherd), Isa 40:11. A flock of one hundred sheep is a modest but viable holding for a Galilean small farmer-shepherd. The leaving of the ninety-nine “in the open country” (en tē erēmō) would have raised a mild objection: are the ninety-nine safe? But the parable focuses on the seeking, not the management of the majority.
15.5: “Joyfully puts it on his shoulders” — the Good Shepherd carrying the found sheep is one of the oldest images in Christian art, derived from this passage. Carrying a found animal was a practical necessity (a frightened or injured sheep would not walk reliably); it was also an act of personal commitment and care that went beyond simple retrieval. The “joyfully” (chairōn) names the emotional register: the finding produces joy before any subsequent celebration.
15.6: “He calls his friends and neighbors together and says, Rejoice with me” — the community celebration is a deliberate social act: the loss and recovery of a sheep would be known throughout a small Palestinian village; the call to shared rejoicing turns private recovery into communal celebration. The “Rejoice with me” formula (sygcharētē moi) echoes the angelic announcement pattern (1:58: neighbors and relatives rejoiced with Elizabeth) and anticipates v.9 (same formula for the coin).
15.7: “More joy in heaven over one sinner who repents than over ninety-nine righteous persons who do not need to repent” — the divine preference for the found-and-returned over the maintained-and-stable; the “righteous who do not need to repent” is gently ironic (everyone needs repentance; but those content with their righteousness generate less divine celebration than the genuinely returning). The “heaven rejoicing” over repentance is expressed in rabbinic literature: b. Sanhedrin 99b describes divine joy over the returning sinner.
15.8: The lost coin parable — the woman’s ten silver drachmas; a drachma was roughly a day’s wage. Some interpreters identify the ten coins as a bride-price or dowry head-chain worn by married women in Palestinian custom (the 'isah), giving the loss a marriage-covenant significance. Whether or not this is the specific reference, the point is that the coin has sentimental or economic value disproportionate to its market worth. The lighting of a lamp, sweeping, and careful search (epistatōs, diligently) are the practical steps of someone who takes the loss seriously.
15.9: The community celebration — “she calls her friends and neighbors together and says, Rejoice with me” — exact parallel to v.6 (shepherd’s celebration). The pattern of the two parables is structurally identical: loss → diligent search → finding → joy → community celebration → divine application. The identical structure reinforces the single theological point: God’s response to recovering the lost is celebratory joy that invites the whole community to share. The Pharisees’ murmuring is the refusal to join this community celebration.
15.10: “Rejoicing in the presence of the angels of God” (charā ginetai enōpion tōn aggelōn tou theou) — not the angels themselves rejoicing but God rejoicing before his angelic court; the formulation is a circumlocution for “God rejoices.” The divine throne room as the scene of celebration when a sinner returns is the eschatological reality that the earthly dinner-party celebration (Jesus eating with sinners) images. The Pharisees who murmur are refusing to participate in heaven’s joy.
15.11: “There was a man who had two sons” — the third and climactic parable of the trilogy; both sons are “lost” in different ways: the younger in a far country through rebellion, the elder in the father’s house through resentment. The two-sons structure echoes the two-son patterns of the OT: Cain and Abel, Ishmael and Isaac, Esau and Jacob, the sons of Jacob; in each case the expected primogeniture order is disrupted. The parable maps onto the situation of ch.15: the sinners (younger son) and the Pharisees (elder son) both in relation to the Father (God).
15.12: “Give me my share of the estate” — demanding inheritance while the father is still alive was possible in Jewish law (m. BB 8:7) and Sirach (33:20–24 warns fathers against giving control of their property during their lifetime). But culturally it was an aggressive and shaming act, practically equivalent to wishing the father dead. The father’s compliance without protest or negotiation is not legally coerced but is a striking voluntary act of love that the elder son will later resent.
15.13: “Squandered his wealth in wild living” (asōtōs, wastefully, recklessly) — the word asōtos (from a-sōzō, cannot be saved/preserved) describes the irreversible dissipation of resources. “A distant country” (chōran makran) — geographically and culturally distant, presumably Gentile; in a Gentile country, Jewish religious and dietary obligations are practically impossible to maintain. The son’s departure is not just economic rebellion but the beginning of religious defilement.
15.14: “A severe famine in that whole country” — famine in the ancient Mediterranean was a regular occurrence tied to harvest failure and drought; the son’s vulnerability was compounded by being without family network in a foreign land. The shame-spiral accelerates: his “coming to need” (hysterēthenai) is the technical term for lacking necessities. The severity of the need is measured by the pig-feeding job of v.15.
15.15: “Hired himself out to a citizen of that country who sent him to feed pigs” — working with pigs for a Gentile employer was the maximum ritual defilement for a Jew; pigs were unclean animals (Lev 11:7; Deut 14:8), and proximity to them was impure. The son has reached the nadir of both economic destitution and ritual impurity. The “citizen of that country” (politēn tēs chōras ekeinēs) is a Gentile; the son is now a client of a Gentile pagan in Gentile territory, tending unclean animals.
15.16: “Longed to fill his stomach with the pods that the pigs were eating” — the pods (keration) are carob beans (Ceratonia siliqua), the locust-bean tree’s fruit, which was cheap animal fodder. The son’s hunger has reached the point where he covets the food reserved for pigs. “No one gave him anything” (oudėis edidou autō) — the social isolation is complete; he has no patron, no family, no community; the Gentile employer provides no welfare. The phrase echoes the rich man and Lazarus (16:21) where the dogs attend Lazarus but “no one gave him anything.”
15.17: “When he came to his senses” (eis heauton elthōn, coming to himself) — the Greek idiom for returning to right mind; the son’s dissipation was a kind of self-alienation or self-abandonment, and his recovery begins with self-knowledge. “My father’s hired servants have food to spare” — the contrast between the father’s household abundance (even the lowest workers have surplus) and the son’s starvation in the far country; the argument is purely pragmatic at this stage: he is hungry and there is food at home.
15.18: The rehearsed speech — “Father, I have sinned against heaven and against you” — the dual sin-confession echoes Exod 10:16 (Pharaoh: “I have sinned against the LORD your God and against you”) and acknowledges both the vertical (against heaven = against God) and horizontal (against the father) dimensions of his wrong. The son’s formulation is theologically accurate: his rebellion against his father was simultaneously rebellion against the God who established the family covenant-structure.
15.19: “Make me like one of your hired servants” (poiēson me hōs hena tōn misthion sou) — the misthios (hired servant) had lower status than a doulos (household slave) because he had no permanent household relationship; he was a day-laborer, not a member of the household. The son’s self-humbling requests the lowest rung of the household structure — not restoration as a son but mere employment. His calculation is still pragmatic: he wants food and safety, not necessarily relationship.
15.20: “While he was still a long way off, his father saw him—was filled with compassion—ran to him—threw his arms around him—kissed him” — five rapid actions before any speech; the father’s response is preemptive, physical, and un-calculating. That the father “saw him while still a long way off” implies he had been watching, perhaps from the roof or the elevated gate. Running was socially undignified for a Palestinian patriarch (required gathering the robe, exposing the legs); the father’s run is a deliberate self-lowering for the sake of the returning son.
15.21: The son begins his rehearsed speech — “Father, I have sinned against heaven and against you. I am no longer worthy to be called your son” — but the “make me like one of your hired servants” request (from v.19) is never spoken; the father interrupts before it reaches the request. The father’s interruption prevents the son from completing the self-degradation that would make him a servant rather than a son; the restoration is given before it is asked for.
15.22: The three restoration gifts — the best robe (stōlēn tēn prōtēn), a ring, sandals — are three status-restoration symbols. The best robe: covering nakedness and shame, marking household membership (cf. the robe given to the returning Esau, Gen 27:27–28 is a stretch, but robes as status markers are pervasive in OT narrative). The signet ring (daktylion): authority and family identity (cf. the ring given to Joseph, Gen 41:42). The sandals: free persons wore sandals; slaves went barefoot (the same sandal distinction appears in John the Baptist’s saying about Jesus’s sandals, 3:16).
15.23: “Bring the fattened calf” (ton moschon ton siteuton) — the specially fattened animal reserved for major celebrations; a household that raised a calf specifically for a feast had been anticipating some occasion of exceptional rejoicing. The immediacy (“quick!” in v.22) and the fattened calf suggest that the father’s preparations for celebration had an anticipatory quality — as if the return had been awaited. The “feast and celebrate” (phagontes euphranthōmen) is the same verb used for the rich man’s daily luxury (16:19: euphrainomenos kath’ hēmeran).
15.24: “This son of mine was dead and is alive again; he was lost and is found” — the resurrection-language applied to the return from the far country; the son was effectively dead (to the family, to his covenant community, to himself) and is now alive. The dead-and-alive, lost-and-found formula is the theological core of the trilogy: it explains why heaven rejoices (vv.7,10) and why the father must celebrate. Death reversed by resurrection requires celebration; to refuse to celebrate is to be indifferent to resurrection.
15.25: The elder son “in the field” — working while the celebration is happening; his distance from the house is spatial but also relational. The elder son’s presence in the field is appropriate (he is the dutiful, working son) but also positions him as an outsider to the house-celebration that is happening without his knowledge or consent. The overheard music and dancing (symphōnias kai chorōn) — the celebration is full-bodied, joyful, and audible from outside.
15.26: The elder son calls a servant to inquire — he does not enter the house to find out directly; his distance from the father is already visible. The servant’s report will be his first information about his brother’s return. The use of a servant as intermediary rather than approaching the father directly anticipates the elder son’s overall posture of distance — he relates to the household through its formal structures rather than through personal relationship with the father.
15.27: The servant’s report: “Your brother has come… your father has killed the fattened calf because he has him back safe and sound (hygiainom)” — the servant frames it as good news; the healthy return of the younger son is the cause of the celebration. The elder son’s response will reveal that he does not share the servant’s evaluation. The use of “your brother” by the servant (vs. the elder son’s later “this son of yours” in v.30) marks the contrast in relational framing.
15.28: “The older brother became angry and refused to go in” (ōrgisthē kai ouk ēthelen eiselthein) — the anger parallels the host’s anger in the Great Banquet parable (v.21), but with the opposite target: the host’s anger is at the refusers; the elder son’s anger is at the welcoming. The refusal to enter the feast is the elder son’s counterpart to the younger son’s departure to the far country; both are forms of separation from the father. “His father went out and pleaded with him” (parēkalei auton) — the father does for the elder son exactly what he did for the younger son: he comes out to meet him.
15.29: “All these years I’ve been slaving for you” (tosauta etē douleū) — the elder son describes his service as slavery (douleo), not sonship; he has related to the father through performance-obligation rather than love. “Never disobeyed your orders” — the obedience is real but its motivation is now revealed as contractual, not relational. “You never gave me even a young goat” — the contrast with the fattened calf; the elder son’s resentment reveals that he has been keeping score, calculating the return on his service.
15.30: “This son of yours” (ho huios sou houtos) — the elder son refuses to say “my brother”; he has disinherited him relationally while the father has reinstated him legally. The added accusation “with prostitutes” (meta pornōn) is not in the original narrative (the parable only says “wild living” in v.13); the elder son’s hostile embellishment may be his assumption or his desire to make the case against the restoration as strong as possible.
15.31: “You are always with me, and everything I have is yours” — the father’s gentle correction identifies the elder son’s category error: he has had continuous access to the father and all the father’s resources, but has not experienced them as gift. He has lived as a servant “earning” what is already fully his by being. The contrast with the younger son is that the elder son’s lostness is interior — he is present in the house but absent from the father’s heart; the younger son was absent from the house but is now found in the father’s embrace.
15.32: “We had to celebrate and be glad, because this brother of yours was dead and is alive again; he was lost and is found” — the father corrects the elder son’s language (“this brother of yours,” restoring the kinship the elder son severed) and repeats the dead-and-alive, lost-and-found formula of v.24. The “had to” (edei) is the necessity language used for the passion (9:22); the celebration of the resurrection-return is a divine necessity. The parable ends without reporting the elder son’s response — an open ending that invites the Pharisees (the story’s auditors) to decide whether they will enter the feast.

16.19: The parable of the Rich Man and Lazarus (16:19-31) is Luke's unique social-reversal parable with an afterlife scene. The rich man's clothing (purple and fine linen) are the markers of extreme wealth in antiquity (purple dye was extraordinarily expensive; fine linen was the priestly and royal cloth). Lazarus (the only named character in a Jesus parable) is a name meaning God has helped — the divine help he received was absent in life but present in death. The parable concludes with the theological point that Abraham's bosom (the Jewish term for the blessed state of the righteous after death) is not available to those who ignore the Law and the Prophets (v.29,31) — reinforcing Luke's consistent theme of wealth as a spiritual danger.
16.1: The Dishonest Manager parable (16:1–13) is unique to Luke and addressed to the disciples (not the Pharisees, who reappear in v.14). The parable’s setting is the Palestinian large-estate economy: a wealthy landowner employs an oikonomos (estate manager/steward) who manages the master’s debtors and accounts. The accusation of “wasting possessions” (diaskorpizōn) uses the same verb as the prodigal son’s waste (15:13), connecting the two parables.
16.2: “Give an account of your management” (apodos ton logon tēs oikonomias sou) — the rendering of accounts was a formal legal procedure; the steward is being dismissed and must account for all his transactions. The finality of “you cannot be manager any longer” means his window to act is the period between the accounting order and the final audit. The steward’s problem is both immediate (loss of employment) and future (no means of support after dismissal).
16.3: The steward’s self-assessment — “I’m not strong enough to dig, I’m ashamed to beg” — reveals class consciousness: manual labor (skaptō, digging) was associated with slaves and the poor; begging was social death for a person of any standing in the Mediterranean world. The steward’s predicament is that he has a white-collar skill set (accounts, relationships, negotiation) but is losing the position from which those skills were exercised.
16.4: “I know what I’ll do so that, when I lose my job here, people will welcome me into their homes” — the steward’s plan is to create social obligations among the master’s debtors by reducing their debts; when he loses his position, these debtors will owe him personal hospitality. The goal is not escape but relational capital — transforming economic transactions into social bonds. This is the Mediterranean patronage system’s logic applied deliberately and strategically.
16.5: The steward summons each debtor individually — the private one-on-one transaction ensures no debtor knows what others are being offered. The master’s debtors are agricultural tenants or merchants who owe the estate produce; their debts are substantial (100 bath-measures of oil, 100 kor of wheat). The steward still has access to the accounts and the legal authority to modify them before his dismissal is finalized; this is the window he exploits.
16.6: One hundred bath-measures of oil reduced to fifty — the batos was approximately 8 gallons; 100 bath was roughly 800 gallons of olive oil, a major agricultural commodity. The reduction of 50% is substantial. Some interpreters identify the removed portion as the steward’s own commission or interest rate that he was adding to the master’s bills — making the reduction not fraud against the master but self-sacrifice of the steward’s own profit margin. This interpretation makes the master’s subsequent commendation less scandalous.
16.7: One hundred kor of wheat reduced to eighty — the koros was approximately 10-12 bushels; 100 kor was a very large wheat debt. The 20% reduction is smaller in percentage than the oil reduction (50%) but larger in absolute terms. If the removed portions represent the steward’s own surcharges, he is buying future hospitality by sacrificing his own income. The parallel structure of the two transactions (bill alteration, quick document-writing) emphasizes the steward’s shrewd, decisive action under time pressure.
16.8: “The master commended the dishonest manager because he had acted shrewdly” — the master’s commendation is the most difficult element of the parable. The commendation is for phrōnimōs (shrewdly, practically-wisely) acting, not for honesty. “The people of this world are more shrewd in dealing with their own kind than are the people of the light” — the disciples are being challenged to deploy equal prudence and strategic thinking for the kingdom that the world’s people deploy for their economic interests.
16.9: “Use worldly wealth (mamōna tēs adikias) to gain friends for yourselves” — mamōnas is the Aramaic word for wealth/property (not pejorative in itself); “of unrighteousness” qualifies it as belonging to the fallen age. The instruction is to use money the way the steward used it: to create relationships and obligations that will outlast the money. “When it is gone, you will be welcomed into eternal dwellings” (aionious skēnās) — the eternal equivalent of the steward’s “welcome me into their homes” (v.4).
16.10: “Whoever can be trusted with very little can also be trusted with much” — the faithfulness-scales principle moves from the steward’s situation to a general principle about character: trustworthiness is a consistent character quality that shows itself in small and large contexts alike. The Mishnah (Avot 4:2: “one mitzvah leads to another mitzvah”) applies the same principle to obedience; faithfulness in small things is the training ground for trustworthiness in large things.
16.11: “If you have not been trustworthy in handling worldly wealth, who will trust you with true riches?” — the contrast between mamōnas (worldly wealth) and “true riches” (alēthinon) frames money as a test-case for handling the real thing. In the Jewish wisdom tradition, wisdom and the fear of the Lord are the true riches (Prov 3:14; 8:10–11; Sir 4:1–10). Faithfulness in the economy of this age is the prerequisite for participation in the economy of the age to come.
16.12: “Trustworthy with someone else’s property” — worldly wealth is framed as allotrion (another’s property, belonging to someone else), not truly yours. The steward’s situation illustrates this: he managed wealth that was not his; his failure was that he wasted it as if it were his own. The disciple who manages worldly wealth faithfully demonstrates the character needed for “property of your own” (hymeteran, your own) — the eschatological inheritance of the kingdom.
16.13: “No one can serve two masters” — the slave-owner social structure makes the metaphor concrete: a slave owned by two masters would have divided loyalties in every specific task; the masters’ competing claims would make every decision a conflict. Mamōnas as a quasi-divine power competing with God for ultimate allegiance is the Lukan wealth theology underlying chs. 12, 16, 18–19; the conclusion is that the disciples must choose their master — not manage a compromise between two absolute claims.
16.14: The Pharisees’ love of money (philargyroi, money-loving) is Luke’s characterization that connects the teaching of vv.1–13 to the following controversy. Their sneering (exemuktērizon, turned up their noses, lit. mocked with the nose) echoes Ps 22:7 (the sufferer mocked by those who stick out their lips and shake their heads). Josephus does not particularly associate Pharisees with wealth; Luke’s characterization may reflect the critique that the Pharisees’ purity and tithing system benefited the wealthy who could afford to maintain it.
16.15: “You are the ones who justify yourselves in the eyes of others, but God knows your hearts” — the contrast between outward self-justification (dikaiountes heautous enōpion tōn anthrōpōn) and divine heart-knowledge. “God knows your hearts” (kardiognōstēs) is an explicit divine attribute (Acts 1:24; 15:8 apply it to God and Jesus). “What people value highly is detestable in God’s sight” (bdelygma enōpion tou theou) — the OT language of idolatry applied to the wealth-prestige system: the love of money is a form of idolatry.
16.16: “The Law and the Prophets were proclaimed until John. Since that time, the good news of the kingdom of God is being proclaimed” — Luke’s clearest statement of the salvation-historical turning point; Acts 13:24–25 and 19:4 use John as the hinge of the two eras. “Everyone is forcing their way into it” (pas eis autēn biazetai) — the kingdom is being entered with urgency and force; the passive may be “everyone is being urged into it” or the active “everyone is pressing into it violently.” Luke’s context suggests the urgency of the kingdom proclamation: the open door is being pressed through.
16.17: “It is easier for heaven and earth to disappear than for the least stroke of a pen to drop out of the Law” — the permanent validity of Torah is asserted immediately after the announcement of a new salvation-historical era. The “least stroke of a pen” (the keraia, the smallest mark in a Hebrew letter, equivalent to a serif) echoes Matt 5:18’s “not one jot or tittle.” Luke’s placement between v.16 (kingdom-era) and v.18 (divorce) suggests that the kingdom does not nullify Torah but fulfills and intensifies it.
16.18: The divorce saying — brief and without exception clause (unlike Matt 5:32’s “except for marital unfaithfulness”) — is placed between the Torah-permanence saying (v.17) and the Rich Man and Lazarus parable (v.19–31). The context is the Law’s application in the kingdom age: Jesus’s one-flesh teaching (Gen 2:24) makes divorce-and-remarriage equivalent to adultery. The Pharisaic school of Hillel permitted divorce on relatively broad grounds (Deut 24:1, “something objectionable”); the school of Shammai permitted it only for sexual immorality; Jesus’s ruling is stricter than either.
16.20: Lazarus at the gate (pylōn, the formal entrance to a substantial house) — the only named character in a Jesus parable; the name “Lazarus” (El’azar, God has helped) is a theological irony: the one whose very name declares divine help receives none from the rich man at whose gate he lies. The gate (pylon) in an ancient Mediterranean city was the public face of the household’s wealth and status; Lazarus’s placement there makes the rich man’s failure to help inescapably deliberate.
16.21: “Even the dogs came and licked his sores” — dogs (kynaria) in antiquity were not domestic pets but scavengers; they gathered wherever there was food, refuse, or disease. Their licking of Lazarus’s sores is not comfort but further degradation — the contact of unclean animals with his wounds. The table-scraps (psichiōn, crumbs) that fall from the rich man’s table are the minimum of social charity; the contrast with the “fine food” of the rich man’s daily feasting makes the neglect inexcusable. “No one gave him anything” echoes 15:16 (the prodigal’s situation).
16.22: “Angels carried him to Abraham’s side” (eis ton kolpon Abraam) — Abraham’s bosom/lap was the Jewish term for the blessed state after death; Josephus describes the righteous as resting “in the bosom of Abraham” (War 2.155). The angelic escort of the righteous soul to Paradise is found in T. Asher 6:4–6 and other Second Temple sources. The rich man’s burial (etaphe) is mentioned in contrast: an elaborately prepared earthly burial vs. an angelic escort to heavenly rest — the final inversion of their earthly positions.
16.23: Hades — the Greek term for the underworld (translating Hebrew Sheol); in Second Temple Jewish thought, Hades contained both the righteous and wicked in separate sections (4 Ezra 7:85–87; 1 En. 22:9–14; Luke 23:43 uses “paradise” for the blessed section). The rich man can see Lazarus in the distance — the reversal is visible: the beggar who lay at the gate, unseen in his need, is now seen in Abraham’s embrace; the rich man who feasted in visible luxury now suffers in visible torment.
16.24: “Father Abraham, have pity on me” — the rich man’s appeal to Abraham as “father” invokes Jewish ethnic identity: John the Baptist had warned that claiming Abraham as father was insufficient (3:8). The request for Lazarus to bring water (a single drop on the tongue) reveals the rich man’s continued assumption that Lazarus is available for errand-running — the persistence of the patron-client power structure that characterized his earthly life. He still thinks of Lazarus as below him, a person to be sent.
16.25: Abraham’s reply “Son, remember” (teknon, mnēsthēti) — the address as “son” (teknon) confirms the genetic-covenantal relationship while explaining the reversal. The principle is the Lukan Beatitudes and Woes in concentrated form: you received your good things, Lazarus received bad things; now the positions are reversed. The finality of the eschatological reversal is the counterpart to the Magnificat’s vision (1:52–53): what God will do, he has now permanently done.
16.26: “A great chasm has been set in place” (chasma mega estēriktai) — the verb stērizō (to fix firmly, establish permanently) — the same word used for Jesus “setting his face” toward Jerusalem (9:51) and for the Tower of Siloam (13:4). The permanence of the post-mortem state is the parable’s structural claim: the reversals of the eschaton are fixed and irreversible. This is not systematic afterlife theology but a warning embedded in a parable: the eschatological reversal is real and final.
16.27: The second request — “send Lazarus to my family” — shifts from personal relief to prophetic warning for the five brothers still alive. Having failed to get relief, the rich man asks for a resurrection-appearance to warn his kin. The motivation may be genuine concern for his brothers — possibly the first moment of care for others he has expressed — or it may be a continuing use of Lazarus as an instrument. Either way, the request reveals the rich man’s continued assumption that miraculous intervention is what converts people.
16.28: “Warn them, so that they will not also come to this place of torment” — the five brothers represent those still alive who have the same wealth and same blindness as their dead brother. The protective motivation mirrors the OT prophetic concern for the household (Ezek 3:17–19; 33:7–9: the watchman must warn). But the mechanism proposed (a resurrection-appearance) is precisely what the rich man thinks will accomplish what Torah and the Prophets have failed to do.
16.29: “They have Moses and the Prophets; let them listen to them” — Abraham’s answer asserts the radical sufficiency of scripture. The Torah and the Prophets contain everything needed to produce the repentance and generosity that would have prevented the rich man’s situation. Deut 15:7–11 (open your hand to the poor), Isa 58:6–7 (share your food with the hungry), Ezek 16:49 (the sin of Sodom: failure to help the poor and needy) — the scripture is not silent about economic obligation to Lazarus-figures.
16.30: “If someone from the dead goes to them, they will repent” — the rich man’s assumption is that a miraculous sign — a resurrection-appearance — would accomplish what scripture has not. This assumption lies behind many requests for signs throughout Luke (11:16,29; 23:8). The assumption is also the Sadducean position in reverse: those who deny resurrection think a resurrection-appearance would be overwhelming proof; the parable’s answer challenges both the assumption that extraordinary signs convert and the premise that scripture is insufficient.
16.31: “If they do not listen to Moses and the Prophets, they will not be convinced even if someone rises from the dead” — the conclusion is a direct Lukan pointer to the resurrection of Jesus: those who rejected the OT witness (the leaders of Israel who knew Moses and the Prophets) were not convinced by the resurrection of Jesus (Acts 2–5 documents the rejection). The parable’s final word frames the entire Gospel’s narrative arc: the resurrection will happen, and some will not believe it because they have already refused to hear what Moses and the Prophets said about it.

19.1: Jericho (19:1-10, Zacchaeus) is the last stop before Jerusalem on the road up from the Jordan Valley. Zacchaeus as chief tax collector (architelones) — a title not found elsewhere in the NT — suggests he was the head contractor for the Jericho district, which would have been profitable: Jericho was a wealthy oasis city with balsam groves, a major product of the region. His height problem and the sycamore tree (ficus sycomorus, a low-branching tree common in the Jordan Valley) are vivid geographic details. Zacchaeus's offer of fourfold restitution and 50% distribution to the poor exceeds what repentance required: the Mosaic law required 20% addition (Lev 6:5, Num 5:7) and fourfold only for stolen animals (Exod 22:1).
19.2: Zacchaeus as chief tax collector (architelōnēs — unique NT title suggesting he was the head tax contractor for the district) would have managed a network of subordinate collectors. Jericho's position on the main trade route from the Jordan ford made it extremely lucrative — balsam exports, date palm trade, and transit taxes from Peraea. His wealth (v.2 'he was rich') is consistent with controlling such a profitable collection point. Tax collectors were despised in Jewish society both for collaboration with Rome and for common fraud (cf. John 2:13-16 on temple money-changers).
19.3: Zacchaeus's desire to see who Jesus was (tis estin houtos) uses an indirect question implying curiosity about identity — not merely about seeing the physical person. The crowd obstacle functions thematically: the very community that benefits from his tax system now physically blocks his access to Jesus. His short stature (mikros tē hēlikia) created a practical problem in the dense crowd typical of first-century Jericho's commercial districts. The detail is realistic rather than merely literary.
19.4: The sycamore-fig tree (sykomorea — Ficus sycomorus, different from the western sycamore) was common in the Jericho valley — a spreading tree with low horizontal branches easily climbed. It was known as food for the poor (Amos 7:14, where Amos tends sycamore-figs) and was associated with humble origins. A wealthy architelōnēs climbing a tree in public — abandoning all dignity — was a comic scene for any Mediterranean observer. The honor-shame violation (a rich man climbing a tree to see a passing teacher) underlines the desperation of his desire.
19.5: 'Zacchaeus, hurry and come down, for I must stay at your house today' — the divine necessity (dei) governing Jesus's choice of Zacchaeus's house is the same word governing the passion (9:22) and Jerusalem destination (13:33). The 'must' is theological: this is the divine itinerary. The invitation to stay (meinai — the abiding/dwelling word used also in John's Gospel for covenantal relationship) in a tax collector's house violated Pharisaic purity norms about table fellowship with sinners (cf. 7:34 — 'friend of tax collectors and sinners').
19.6: 'He hurried and came down and received him joyfully' (chaírōn) — the joy-word (chairō) is a signal of kingdom reception in Luke: the same root appears in the Annunciation (1:14), the angel's proclamation (2:10), the disciples' return (10:17), and the prodigal son's reception (15:32). Zacchaeus's joy at receiving Jesus characterizes him as the 'good soil' of the sower parable (8:13). The speed of response ('hurried') mirrors the urgency of the divine invitation.
19.7: 'When they saw it, they all grumbled' (diagongyzō — the same verb used for Israel's wilderness murmuring against God in the LXX: Exod 16:7-8; Num 11:1; 14:2) — Luke's use of the exodus-murmuring verb for the crowd's reaction to Jesus's table-fellowship choices is deliberate.
